coredata.dc:title: Folic acid functionalized gadolinium-doped carbon dots as fluorescence / magnetic resonance imaging contrast agent for targeted imaging of liver cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Jinglei
Zhou, Shizhao
Ma, Yihua
Wei, Yingying
Li, Qiang
Huang, Hui
Chen, Lin
Yang, Yongzhen
Yu, Shiping
coredata.dc:description: Gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs), as a new class of nanomaterial, has a wide application prospect in targeted imaging and monitoring diagnosis and treatment of liver cancer because of their good fluorescence (FL)-magnetic resonance (MR) imaging properties. First, Gd-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with gadodiamide as gadolinium source, citric acid as carbon source and silane coupling agent (KH-792) as coupling agent with FL quantum yield (QY) of 48.2%. Then, folic acid (FA), which is highly expressed in liver cancer, was used as a targeting component to modify Gd-CDs to obtain targeted imaging agent (Gd-CDs-FA). The results showed that Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, and the targeting and selectivity of Gd-CDs-FA to HepG2 cells could be observed under confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The T1 longitudinal relaxation rates (r1) of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA are 15.92 mM−1s−1 and 13.56 mM−1s−1, respectively. They showed good MR imaging ability in vitro and in vivo, and MR imaging in nude mice further proved the targeting imaging performance of Gd-CDs-FA. Therefore, Gd-CDs-FA with higher QY showed good FL-MR targeting imaging ability of liver cancer, which broke through the limitations of single molecular imaging probe in sensitivity and soft tissue resolution. This study provides a new idea for the application of Gd-CDs in FL and MR targeting imaging of liver cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent imaging
Magnetic resonance imaging
Targeted imaging of liver cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro MRI and relaxivity study
Cytotoxicity and tissue and organ toxicity
In vitro and vivo MRI study

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China [ 82172048, U21A20378 ], Shanxi Center of Technology Innovation for Controlled and Sustained Release of Nano-drugs [ 202104010911026 ], Foundational Research Project of Shanxi Province [ 202103021223439; 202203021211159 ], Shanxi-Zheda Institute of Advanced Materials and Chemical Engineering [ 2021SX-FR010 ], Shanxi Scholarship Council of China [ 2022-039 ], Four “Batches” Innovation Project of Invigorating Medical through Science and Technology of Shanxi Province ( 2023XM012 ), Take-off Foundation of Shanxi Cancer Hospital for Innovation Team ( TD2023003 ), Companion Flight Foundation of Shanxi Cancer Hospital for Doctoral Supervisors ( BD2023004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Gd-CDs, (b) size distribution of Gd-CDs, (c) TEM image of Gd-CDs-FA, (d) size distribution of Gd-CDs-FA, inset of (a, b) are the HRTEM images. (e) XRD pattern of Gd-CDs, (f) FT-IR spectra of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA, and (g) zeta potential of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA and FA.
(a) Full scan XPS spectrum. (b-f) high-resolution XPS spectra for Gd3d, C1s, O1s, Si2p and N1s respectively.
(a) UV–vis spectra of FA, Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA. (b) PL spectra of Gd-CDs at different excitation wavelength. (c) PL of Gd-CDs-FA.

(a) Photographs of red blood cells after treatment by Gd-CDs (a1) and Gd-CDs-FA (a2) at different concentrations and Triton X-100 solutions. (b) Hemolysis of red blood cells in the presence of different concentration of Gd-CDs. (c) Hemolysis of red blood cells in the presence of different concentration of Gd-CDs-FA.

(a) Cytotoxicity of Gd-CDs against HL-7702, HeLa and HepG2 cells at increasing concentration from 0 to 400 μg/mL. (b) Cytotoxicity of Gd-CDs-FA against HL-7702, HeLa and HepG2 cells at increasing concentration from 0 to 400 μg/mL. (c) Histological changes of different organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) in mice after injection of PBS, Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA solutions for one week. (d) The body weight change trend of mice in three groups after injection of PBS, Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solutions for one week. The scale bar is 25 µm.
The bright field, fluorescence and merged images of HepG2 cells incubated with 200 μg/mL Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA for 4 h were observed under CLSM. (scale bar=50 µm).

(a) Linear relationship between 1/T1 and different concentrations of Gd3+. (b) T1 weighted images of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA at different concentration. (c) T1-weighted images of tumor-bearing mice at different time points (40, 100, 160 and 220 min) before and after injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA, the yellow and blue arrows point to the tumor. (d) Changes of SNR value in two groups of mice before and after injection in 220 min.
Schematic diagram of hydrothermal synthesis of Gd-CDs as a FL-MR probe.
Synthesis methods, feedstocks, and characterization results of Gd-doped CDs.

row.entry: Sucrose, concentrated H2SO4, GdCl3, and Diethylene glycol

head.ce:title: Folic acid functionalized gadolinium-doped carbon dots as fluorescence / magnetic resonance imaging contrast agent for targeted imaging of liver cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Province Cancer Hospital, Shanxi Hospital Affiliated to Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Cancer Hospital Affiliated to Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Honghui Hospital of Xi’ an Jiaotong University, Xi’ an 710054, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interventional Treatment Department, Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Interventional Treatment Department, Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Taiyuan University of Technology, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Taiyuan University of Technology, Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Taiyuan University of Technology, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered as co-first authors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs), as a new class of nanomaterial, has a wide application prospect in targeted imaging and monitoring diagnosis and treatment of liver cancer because of their good fluorescence (FL)-magnetic resonance (MR) imaging properties. First, Gd-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with gadodiamide as gadolinium source, citric acid as carbon source and silane coupling agent (KH-792) as coupling agent with FL quantum yield (QY) of 48.2%. Then, folic acid (FA), which is highly expressed in liver cancer, was used as a targeting component to modify Gd-CDs to obtain targeted imaging agent (Gd-CDs-FA). The results showed that Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, and the targeting and selectivity of Gd-CDs-FA to HepG2 cells could be observed under confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The T1 longitudinal relaxation rates (r1) of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA are 15.92 mM−1s−1 and 13.56 mM−1s−1, respectively. They showed good MR imaging ability in vitro and in vivo, and MR imaging in nude mice further proved the targeting imaging performance of Gd-CDs-FA. Therefore, Gd-CDs-FA with higher QY showed good FL-MR targeting imaging ability of liver cancer, which broke through the limitations of single molecular imaging probe in sensitivity and soft tissue resolution. This study provides a new idea for the application of Gd-CDs in FL and MR targeting imaging of liver cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Liver cancer is one of the most common malignant tumors. In recent years, the incidence and mortality of liver cancer are on the rise. With the continuous exploration of the diagnosis and treatment of liver cancer, the imaging diagnosis technology is also improved, which is of great significance for the diagnosis and treatment of early liver cancer. At present, there are a variety of diagnostic imaging methods for liver cancer, such as X-ray computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), positron emission computed tomography and so on, but the sensitivity needs to be improved. Among them, MRI has the characteristics of high soft tissue resolution, multi-sequence imaging, no radiation hazard, multi-parameters and so on. In addition, MRI can also provide functional imaging information, such as spectral analysis, diffusion imaging, etc [1,2], which makes a great contribution to the early diagnosis of cancer, so it is widely used in clinical diagnosis, but its targeting and sensitivity are low [3,4]. In contrast, FL has superior sensitivity, but it has low soft tissue resolution and poor spatial penetration, so the combination of FL and MR to construct FL-MR molecular probe can make up for the shortcomings of single imaging technology in spatial resolution and sensitivity, so as to improve the reliability of tumor diagnosis [5].

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used as bioluminescence imaging probes due to their satisfactory FL properties, easily surface functionalized, low cytotoxicity, and superior biocompatibility [6,7]. Gd3+ is a metal ion with strong paramagnetism and shows low toxicity when it exists in the form of chelates. Gd3+ chelate has 7 pairs of electrons, which can produce a strong magnetic moment and significantly shorten the T1 relaxation time. This unique property makes Gd3+ chelate widely used as a MRI contrast agent [5,8,9]. Taking gadodiamide as gadolinium source, citric acid as carbon source, Gd-CDs can be obtained after hydrothermal reaction. In the preparation of Gd-CDs, Gd3+ chelate ring in gadolinium diamine is fractured due to high temperature [10], so that Gd3+ is dissociated from the complex and participates in the carbonization process of CDs. Finally, Gd3+ exists in the form of chelated state in Gd-CDs. The presence of chelated Gd not only makes the heavy metal Gd lose its toxicity, but also endows CDs with MRI properties [11]. Therefore, Gd-CDs has the advantages of both FL and MR imaging, which provides the possibility for real-time diagnosis and monitoring of liver cancer.

However, the disadvantage of most existing Gd-CDs is the poor coordination between quantum yield (QY) and longitudinal relaxation rate r1. Du et al. [12] reported that the QY and r1 of Gd-doped CDs prepared by hydrothermal method are 13.4% and 6.45 mM−1s−1, respectively. Although the QY of Gd-CDs prepared by Zheng et al. [13] is as high as 57.78%, their r1 is low, which is only 6.27 mM−1s−1. Recently, Huang et al. [14] synthesized Gd-CQDs with a QY of 31.78% and a r1 of 5.16 mM−1s−1, both of which were not satisfactory. The above results show that the combination of FL and MR imaging properties will lead to the decrease of FL intensity or MRI ability [15]. Therefore, it is an urgent problem to develop CDs with superior FL and MRI capabilities at the same time. In this study, the QY of Gd-CDs is as high as 48.2% and the r1 is up to 15.92 mM−1s−1. Compared with previous studies, both of the QY and r1 of Gd-CDs are cooperatively improved.

Although Gd-CDs can be retained in the tumor region through passively targeted EPR (Enhanced Permeability and Retention) effect, its passive targeting effect is not specific to liver cancer cells [16]. As the targeting recognition and imaging of liver cancer is the key to early diagnosis, treatment and reducing the side effects of late radiotherapy and chemotherapy [17], it is very important to couple specific liver cancer targeting molecules on the surface of imaging probes. Active targeting of cancer refers to guiding molecular probes loaded with targeted molecules to the corresponding receptors of targeted molecules on the surface of cancer cells, so that probes gather on the surface of cancer cells, thus playing an important role in targeted diagnosis and treatment of cancer [18]. Among them, folic acid (FA) has become a good targeting molecule for liver cancer because of its non-immunogenicity, high stability, small molecular size and active binding to FA receptors overexpressed on the surface of liver cancer cells [19]. Modification of FA on the surface of Gd-CDs can give it the ability to actively target liver cancer cells and realize the targeted diagnosis of liver cancer [20]. Although many FL-MR molecular probes have been developed, up to now, there are few studies on CDs with ability of FL-MR imaging and targeting on liver cancer.

In this work, using citric acid as carbon source, gadolinium diamine as gadolinium source and silane coupling agent (KH-792) as additive, Gd-CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method to enhance the ability of FL and MR imaging (as shown in Scheme1). Moreover, the active targeting of liver cancer was achieved by coupling FA on Gd-CDs to obtain Gd-CDs-FA. The QY of the prepared Gd-CDs was as high as 48.2% and the r1 was 15.92 mM−1s−1. Both Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA showed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. The targeting imaging ability of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA in vitro was verified by HepG2 cells under confocal microscope. Furthermore, the ability of Gd-CDs-FA to target tumor in vivo was demonstrated in liver cancer model mice. It is fully proved that as a new functionalized carbon nanomaterial, Gd-CDs can not only realize the dynamic monitoring of the tumor, but also reduce the toxic and side effects on the surrounding normal tissues, which makes them have excellent application potential in the field of liver cancer targeted imaging and therapy.

Gadodiamide (0.5 mmol/mL, with a purity of 99.02%) was purchased from GE Healthcare Lreland Limited. Citric acid (C6H10O8) and KH-792 were bought from Guangfu Science and Technology Development Co. Ltd. Tianjin. Ethylenediamine was produced by Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. FA was from Maya reagent Co. Ltd. Hepatocyte (HL-7702), liver cancer cell (HepG2) and cervical cancer cell (HeLa) were purchased from Shanghai Fuheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Disposable membrane (0.22 µm) came from Lianghe Glass Instrument Company, Taixing. Dialysis bags with retained molecular weight of 1000 Da was obtained from Shanghai Yuansong Instrument Technology Co. Ltd. Selected standard fetal bovine serum came from from Cellmax. 0.25% trypsin, dual-antibody (penicillin and strepto-mycin), aseptic phosphate buffered saline (PBS), 1640 medium and Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) were purchased from Wuhan BOSTER Biological Technology Co. Ltd.

Gd-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal treatment. 2.0 g of citric acid, 30 mL of gadodiamide and 10 mL of KH-792 were dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water, and then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon autoclave (100 mL). After heating at 200 ℃ for 6 h, it can be seen that the solution changed from colorless to faint yellow. Subsequently, the resultant solution was cooled to room temperature naturally and filtered through filtration membrane (0.22 µm) to remove large particles. Then the filtrate was dialyzed for 24 h in a 1000 Da dialysis bag in super pure water (water was replaced every 4 h). Finally, the transparent yellow solution was freeze dried to obtain powder Gd-CDs.

First, 24 mg of FA, 12 mg of Gd-CDs, 9 mg of EDC, and 9 mg of NHS were dissolved in 30 mL of PBS (pH=7.4), and then stirred in a magnetic agitator at room temperature for 24 h. The γ-carboxylic acid on the surface of FA reacted with the amino group on the surface of Gd-CDs to form an amide bond, which make the FA bond to the surface of CDs. The resultant solution was further filtered with a disposable filtration membrane (0.22 µm), and the filtrate was dialyzed in water for 24 h through a 1000 Da dialysis bag. Finally, the purified aqueous solution of Gd-CDs-FA was freeze-dried.

Transmission images (TEM) and high-resolution transmission image (HRTEM) of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were obtained by a JEOL JEM-2010 microscope at an operating voltage of 200 kV. The particle size analyzer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern, UK) was used to analyze the zeta potential and size distribution. Determination of Gd by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Agilent 5110, Australia). The content of Gd was calculated by the following formula:
(1)
Cx ( mg / kg ) =
C
0
( mg / L ) × f ×
V
0
( mL ) ×
10
− 2
m
g
×
10
− 1
(2)
W
%
=
C
x
mg
/
kg
10
6
× 100 %
where m is the mass of the sample (g), V 0 is the volume of the sample after digestion (mL), f is the dilution factor, C 0 is the concentration of the Gd element in the test solution as measured by the instrument (mg/L). Cx is the concentration of Gd element in every kilogram sample (mg/kg), W(%) is the mass percentage of Gd element. The X-ray diffraction pattern of Gd-CDs was measured by an X-Ray Diffractomer (XRD, DX-2700, China Dandong Fangyuan instrument Co.Ltd.), equipped with 2θ scan mode, Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å), the voltage is 40 kV, and the current is 40 mA. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the products at different stages were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi, Japan). Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) was obtained by using a FL spectrometer (Fmur7000, Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Esclab 250Xi, USA) was conducted to detect the five elements of C, N, O, Gd and Si in Gd-CDs. The change of Gd orbital can be used to determine whether Gd is mixed into CDs. The chemical structure of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were characterized by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Tensor27, Germany). Cytotoxicity was detected by a multifunctional microplate reader (Thermo Fisher, USA). Cell fluorescence imaging was carried out on a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, LSM880, Germany). RadiAnt DICOM Viewer (64-bit) software was used to select the region of interest (ROI) of MRI images.

HL-7702 and HeLa cells were grown in 1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% double antibody. HepG2 cells were cultured in high glucose medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% double antibody. All three kinds of cells were incubated in moist environment of 5% CO2 at 37 ℃.

Fresh human blood was collected and then placed in an anticoagulant tube to avoid clotting. First, the blood was centrifuged at the speed of 1000 rpm/min for 10 min, then washed repeatedly with PBS solution until the supernatant was clear and transparent to obtain clean red blood cells. Subsequently, the obtained red blood cells were configured to be 2% red blood cells suspension with PBS. Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were arranged into equal volume concentration gradient solutions with concentrations of 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL respectively with PBS. Red blood cells suspension was mixed with Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solution as experimental group. The red blood cells suspension mixed with PBS was used as the negative control group, and the red blood cells suspension mixed with 1% TritonX-100 solution as the positive control group. After incubation in a water bath at 37 ℃ for 2 h, the above three groups of solutions were centrifuged at the speed of 1000 rpm/min for 10 min. Finally, the optical density (OD) of the supernatant at 575 nm was measured by UV–vis. Each group of samples had three parallel replicates, the hemolysis rate of the experimental groups was calculated by the following formula:
(3)
Hemolysis ( % ) =
OD ( sample ) − OD ( negative control )
OD
positive control
− OD ( negative control )
× 100 %

The cell viability of HL-7702, HeLa and HepG2 cells cultured in different concentrations of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA for 24 h were studied by CCK-8 assay. First of all, HL-7702, HeLa and HepG2 were fully digested and centrifuged, then added in 4 mL of medium respectively and gently beaten into a uniform cell suspension. Then count the cells under the light microscope, adjust the number of cells to 2.0 × 104/well, absorb 100 μL/well with a micropipette and inoculate it in the 96-well plates. The 96-well plates inoculated with cells were placed in the cell incubator of 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 24 h, and then each well was washed with PBS for 3 times. Subsequently, the Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solutions with concentration gradients of 0, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL were added to each well, and then cultured in the cell incubator of 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 24 h. Finally, the old medium was discarded and washed with PBS for 4 times. 90 μL of the mixture of basic medium and 10 μL of CCK-8 dye were added to each well, and the 96-well plate were cultured in a cell incubator of 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 1.5 h. The OD of each well was measured at 450 nm by a multifunctional microplate reader. The experiment was repeated 3 times independently. Cell viability was calculated by the following equation:
(4)
Cell viability ( % ) =
OD
sample
− OD
blank
)
OD
control
− OD ( blank )
× 100 %

BALB/c nude mice were randomly divided into 3 groups with 3 mice in each group. First, PBS, Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solution (200 μg/mL, 10 mg/kg) were injected into the tail vein and cultured in the animal room at 22 ℃ for 7 days. After 7 days, the three groups of mice were euthanized, and the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and other organs of each mouse were collected to make hematoxylin-eosinstaining (HE) sections, and the histological morphology of sections was observed under a microscope (pictures were taken under a 40-fold lens). At the same time, the growth and development of mice was observed by monitoring the change trend of body weight of three groups of mice in 7 days. All mice in each group drank water and had normal diet for 7 days.

In order to explore the targeting FL imaging ability of Gd-CDs-FA in liver cancer cells, the FL imaging of Gd-CDs in liver cancer cells was used as control. HepG2 cells were first cultured in confocal culture dish for 24 h. When the number of cells grew to 60%∼70% of the dish, the old medium was discarded. Then 200 μg/mL of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were added and placed in a cell incubator at 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for further culture for 4 h. Then 2 mL of 4% paraformaldehyde was added and fixed for 10 min. After discarding the fixed solution, it was washed with PBS for 3 times. Finally, cell images were taken with a CLSM at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro MRI and relaxivity study
ce:section.ce:para: MRI of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA with different concentrations of Gd3+ was investigated using a 7 T MRI scanner (Biospec 7 T/20 USR, Bruker, Germany) in vitro. Different Gd3+ concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6 mM) of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were prepared by diluting them with deionized water. T1 images were collected by T1-RARE sequence, and T1 relaxation time was measured by T1-mapping sequence. The r1 value of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were obtained by calculating the slope of the curve of relaxation rate (1/T1) against Gd3+ concentration. T1 weighted images were acquired under the following parameters: TR/TE = 500/7 ms, FOV = 20 × 20 cm2, slice thickness = 0.7 mm, flip angle = 180°.

BALB/c male mice were used to study the targeting MRI of liver cancer. First of all, 7-week-old nude mice were modeled with liver cancer. The HepG2 cells in the logarithmic growth phase were taken out and the number of cells were adjusted to 5 × 104/200 μL. 200 μL HepG2 cell suspension was injected into the right axilla of BALB/c nude mice. The inoculated mice were fed under standard experimental conditions, and the tumor growth was observed every day. The subcutaneous tumor growth at the injection site was observed about 13 days. When the tumor volume was measured as 200 mm3 with Vernier caliper, it could be used in the experiment.

Nine BALB/c tumor-bearing nude mice were randomly divided into two groups with three mice in each group: one group was injected with Gd-CDs, the other group was injected with Gd-CDs-FA. First, the nude mice in each group were anesthetized by isoflurane gas, and then injected Gd-CDs solution and Gd-CDs-FA solution through the tail vein, respectively. T1 images were collected at 40, 100, 160 and 220 min, respectively, and then the enhanced imaging effects of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA in subcutaneous liver cancer were analyzed. Five layers of the tumor were selected at each time point, and then a circular ROI area with a diameter of about 3 mm was selected at the same layer (avoiding the area with uneven signal) to calculate the average Signal intensity (SI) of each layer. Finally, the signal to noise ratio (SNR) was used to quantitatively analyze the trend of signal changes in the tumor region. The SI of each group was obtained using the same scanner under the same parameters. T1 weighted images were acquired under the following parameters: TR/TE = 500/8 ms, slice thickness = 1.5 mm, flip angle = 180°. SNR values were calculated by the following equation:
SNR (tumor) =SIa/SI (pre)
SIa is the signal intensity of the tumor region at each time point, and SI(Pre) is the signal intensity of the tumor region before injection.

The morphology and particle size of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were observed by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1(a, b), the shape of Gd-CDs is spherical, the particle size is uniform, the average particle size was calculated to be 3.35 nm, and the particle size distribution is relatively narrow. As shown in Fig. 1(c, d), Gd-CDs-FA is spherical with uniform size distribution, and the average particle size is about 5.22 nm. HRTEM images (Fig. 1a, c upper right corner illustration) show that there are no obvious lattice stripes in Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA, which proves that Gd-CDs is amorphous.

The phase structure of Gd-CDs was characterized by XRD. θ is the angle between X-ray and the corresponding crystal plane, and the wide diffraction peak of 2 θ between 20° and 30° corresponds to the diffraction peak of amorphous carbon [21]. As seen in Fig. 1(e), Gd-CDs has only one diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.98°, indicating that the synthesized Gd-CDs is amorphous. The result is consistent with HRTEM, which further verifies the amorphous structure of Gd-CDs.

FT-IR were measured to analyze the surface functional groups of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA, as shown in Fig. 1(f). Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA have a broad absorption peak around 1600 cm−1, which corresponds to CO stretching vibration [22,23]. The distinct absorption bands located at 3425 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of N–H/–OH [24]. The absorption peak at 2924 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration absorption peak of –CH2 group. The stretching vibration of C–O–C is located at 1107 cm−1. The absorption band at 1317 cm−1 is assigned to vibration of C–N. The above results show that there are hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups in Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA [25,26]. According to the FT-IR results of FA reported in the literature [27], there is an absorption peak of FA at 1639 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of –CO–NH– group. In Fig. 1(f), the Gd-CDs-FA has a new absorption peak at 1639 cm−1 compared with Gd-CDs, which is a sign formed by the FA characteristic –CO–NH– function group, confirmed FA combined with the Gd-CDs through the amide bond [28,29].

The zeta potentials of different samples were measured by Nano-ZS90 particle size analyzer. As shown in Fig. 1(g), the zeta potentials of Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA and FA are 9.23, −14.39 and −24.01 mV, respectively. After loading FA, Gd-CDs changed from positive potential to negative potential, which further proved the successful loading of FA.

The content of Gd in Gd-CDs was measured by ICP-AES which is 9.3%. The doping of Gd into CDs was further confirmed by XPS. XPS spectra of Gd-CDs as shown in Fig. 2(a), there are five typical binding energies for Gd-CDs, which are C1s, O1s, N1s, Si2p, Gd3d. As shown in Fig. 2(b), Gd-CDs has two typical binding energies at 1187 eV and 1219 eV, corresponding to chiral coupling Gd3d5/2 and Gd3d3/2. It further confirms that Gd3+ has been doped into Gd-CDs [15,30,31]. In addition, four strong binding energies were observed at 284 eV, 530 eV, 398 eV, and 102 eV, corresponding to the characteristic absorption peaks of C1s, O1s, N1s and Si2p, respectively. The C1s energy spectrum has two absorption peaks at 248.8 eV, and 286 eV, which proves that the element C is mainly in the form of C–C and C═O, respectively in Fig. 3(c). As shown in Fig. 2(d), O is mainly represented by the chemical state of C═O. Si2p has an absorption peak at 102 eV, which proves that Si is mainly in the chemical state of organosilicon, and the N1s energy spectrum has two absorption peaks at 398 eV and 400 eV, which are assigned to N–Si and NSi2O, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(e, f) [32,33]. The existence of these characteristic peaks demonstrated that Gd-CDs is mainly composed of C, N, O, Si, Gd elements.

To explore the optical properties of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA, UV–vis and PL ( Fig. 4) of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA were measured. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FA was measured to investigated the differences before and after its modification on Gd-CDs. FA has a strong absorption peak at 280 and 347 nm. While Gd-CDs has an obvious absorption peak at 245 nm, which is due to the π-π * transition in the C═C [34], and an obvious absorption peak can also be seen at 360 nm, which is attributed to the n-π * transition of C═O [35,36]. Gd-CDs-FA also has strong absorption peaks at 280 and 347 nm, and its strong absorption peak at 280 nm corresponds to the absorption peak originating from FA, indicating that FA is modified on the surface of Gd-CDs.

Fig. 3(b) is the emission spectra of Gd-CDs solution at different excitation wavelengths. A series of emission spectra were obtained by increasing the excitation wavelength of Gd-CDs from 285 to 405 nm, and their maximum emission wavelength are all located at 444 nm, among which the strongest emission intensity is obtained when the excitation wavelength is fixed at 365 nm. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the excitation wavelength of Gd-CDs-FA is increased from 305 to 425 nm, and a series of emission spectra were obtained, their maximum emission wavelength is at 456 nm. The FL emission intensity is the strongest when the excitation wavelength is 405 nm. Under the excitation of the best excitation wavelength, the FL emission intensity is the strongest, that is, the biological imaging intensity is the strongest. Therefore, when the excitation wavelengths of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA are 365 nm and 405 nm respectively, the bioluminescence imaging effect of both is the best. At the same time, compared with Gd-CDs, the maximum emission wavelength of Gd-CDs-FA has a red shift, which may be due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer caused by the π electron shift of FA to Gd-CDs, which further proves the grafting of FA [28]. From the inset the photographs in Fig. 3(b, c), the photographs of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solutions under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp, it can be clearly seen that both of the two solutions emit blue light when excited by 365 nm UV lamp, demonstrating their good properties of FL.

The application of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA in vivo should not only have excellent FL properties and nuclear MRI performance, but also have low toxicity and biocompatibility. If the free Gd3+ in the organism is not metabolized effectively, the accumulation in the body will cause biological pathway toxicity and eventually lead to renal insufficiency. Therefore, it is necessary to study their biocompatibility before using them in human body.

In this study, hemolysis assay with red blood cells was conducted to evaluate the blood compatibility of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA. Fig. 4 (a1, a2) shows the images of red blood cells treated by Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs-FA and Triton X-100 solution with different concentrations. From left to right, the concentrations of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA are 0 (PBS, negative control group), 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL, and Triton X-100 solution (positive control group), respectively. From Fig. 4 (a1, a2), it can be seen that hemolysis occurred in the positive control group, while the red blood cells in the negative control group were intact, and there was no obvious hemolysis in the Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solutions (3.125–400 μg/mL). In order to further explore the hemolysis of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA, the OD value of each supernatant at 575 nm was measured by UV–vis. As shown in Fig. 4(b, c), the hemolysis rates of red blood cells suspensions treated with PBS solution and Triton X-100 are 0% and 100%, respectively, while the hemolysis rates of different concentrations of Gd-CDs solution (3.125–400 μg/mL) are 1.8%, 3.2%, 3.5%, 2.0%, 2.5%, 1.7%, 1.8%, respectively. The hemolysis rates of different concentrations of Gd-CDs-FA solution (3.125–400 μg/mL) were 1.8%, 1.9%, 3.4%, 1.4%, 3.2%, 3.0%, 3.0% and 2.1%, respectively. The results showed that all the hemolysis rate of the experimental group was less than 5%, indicating that Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA have good blood compatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and tissue and organ toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity was determined by CCK-8 method, HL-7702, HeLa and HepG2 cells were incubated with Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solution for 24 h, separately, and then the cell viability was measured. As shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b), the cell viability of each cell in low concentration of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solution was similar to that of the blank control group. When the concentrations of the two solutions were as high as 400 μg/mL, the cell viability of the three kinds of cells remained more than 85%, indicating that there was no significant difference in the cell viability of HL-7702, HeLa and HepG2 cells. These results showed that Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA have low toxicity to the three kinds of cells.

One week after injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solution into tail vein of nude mice, the toxicity of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA was evaluated by histological HE staining sections of different organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney). As shown in Fig. 5(c), the micromorphology and structure of tissues and organs in the experimental group were no different from those in the control group, and there was no tissue injury or necrosis, indicating that Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA do not cause tissue damage. To explore the effects of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA on the growth and development of mice, the change trend of body weights was monitored. The weight monitoring results were shown in Fig. 5(d). The body weight of each group increased normally. There was no weight loss or death, and there was no significant difference in the trend of weight change between the control group and the experiment group. The biosafety of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA in vivo was further verified.

In order to evaluate the ability of Gd-CDs-FA for targeted FL imaging of liver cancer, Gd-CDs was used as the control group and Gd-CDs-FA as the experimental group to study their ability of imaging. CLSM was used to observe the FL imaging of the two probes in HepG2 cells under the excitation wavelength of 405 nm. As shown in Fig. 6, blue fluorescence can be observed in HepG2 cells incubated with Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA. However, HepG2 cells incubated with Gd-CDs-FA show brighter blue fluorescence, which proves that Gd-CDs-FA can be used for targeted imaging of liver cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and vivo MRI study
ce:section.ce:para: The MRI ability was characterized by the longitudinal relaxation rate r1, which is the slope of the curve of relaxation velocity (1/T1) versus Gd3+ concentration. The larger the r1, the stronger the MRI ability [12]. The T1-weighted imaging capabilities of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA are evaluated on a 7 T MRI scanner. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the r1 of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA is 15.92 mM−1s−1 and 13.56 mM−1s−1, respectively. Sun et al. determined that the r1 of gadodiamide for T1 contrast agent is 4.975 mM−1s−1 [37], and the r1 value of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA are much higher than that of gadodiamide. Fig. 7(b) can visually observe that the T1 weighted image signal is increased with the Gd3+ concentration, and it is confirmed that the loading of FA did not have a large impact on the MRI performance of the Gd-CDs. The above results show that Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA can be used as T1 molecular probes for MRI. The larger r1 value may be related to the hydrodynamic radius and surface area of Gd-CDs. Since r1 mainly depends on the external magnetic field and the interaction between protons and surrounding tissues, the higher specific surface area can maximize the interaction between Gd3+ and water protons, which is beneficial to its full contact with water protons to reduce its relaxation time and increase r1 [38].

Male BALB/c nude mice were used to study the targeting MRI of liver cancer. Fig. 7(c) records the cross-sectional T1-weighted images at different time before and after injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA in tumor-bearing mice. The trend of signal changes in the tumor region was measured by SNR value in Fig. 7(d). Before the injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA, there was no significant difference between the signal of normal tissue and the tumor area. When CDs enters the blood circulation after injection through the tail vein, the signal enhancement of tumor is earlier than that of organs and muscles. It can be attribute to the blood supply of tumor cells is abundant and the gap between blood vessels is wide, resulting in EPR effect when the nanoparticles enter into tumor cells through vascular gaps. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the tumor signal began to increase after 40 min was injected into the tail vein, and reached the maximum in both groups after 100 min of injection. Moreover, the tumor signal in Gd-CDs-FA group was significantly higher than that in Gd-CDs group. After injection of 100 min, with the metabolism of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA through blood, the signal value of tumor area began to decrease in both groups, but the signal value in Gd-CDs-FA group was always higher than that in Gd-CDs group, which proved that Gd-CDs-FA were targeted to liver cancer.

To further confirm the superiority of Gd-CDs-FA in FL-MR imaging, it is compared with other Gd-doped CDs from the feedstock, QY, and r1 value (as shown in Table 1). It can be seen that Gd-CDs-FA has a relative high QY and r1 compared with other Gd-doped CDs, which can show better imaging capability.

To sum up, this study reported the application of Gd-CDs-FA in targeting imaging of liver cancer. The targeting FL-MR imaging of liver cancer was realized by chelating Gd3+ into CDs and coupling FA on its surface. Specifically, a simple and rapid synthesis was carried out by hydrothermal method using gadodiamide as gadolinium source, citric acid as carbon source and KH-792 as coupling agent. Gd-CDs with QY of 48.2%, average size of 3.35 nm, zeta potential of 9.23 mV and Gd doping amount of 9.3% was obtained after 5 h reaction at 200 ℃. Then FA was grafted onto the surface of Gd-CDs by amide reaction, and Gd-CDs-FA has an average size of 5.22 nm and zeta potential of −14.39 mV. Both of them showed excellent FL properties, including strong absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region. At the excitation wavelength of 365 and 405 nm, the Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA show the best imaging effect, respectively. It was observed that the fluorescence imaging effect of Gd-CDs-FA on HepG2 cells is stronger than that of Gd-CDs, which proved the targeting effect of Gd-CDs-FA to liver cancer. The CCK-8 study proved that when the Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA solution reached 400 μg/mL, the cell viability was still more than 85%, and the hemolysis assay also proved that both hemolysis rates were less than 5%, indicating that both of them had low cytotoxicity and good blood compatibility. Through the study of MRI properties in vitro, the relaxation rates of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA are 15.92 mM−1s−1 and mM−1s−1, respectively, which are higher than the clinical magnetic contrast agent gadodiamide, indicating that they have good relaxation properties. In the in vivo experiment of nude mice, there was no tissue injury or necrosis in the microstructure of tissues and organs after injection of the two materials, which further proved the compatibility of Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA. Finally, both Gd-CDs and Gd-CDs-FA showed good MRI ability in nude mice, and Gd-CDs-FA showed targeted MRI of hepatoma in nude mice. This study lays a good foundation for targeted FL-MR imaging diagnosis and monitoring of liver cancer.

Chen Lin: Writing – original draft. Yang Yongzhen: Writing – original draft. Huang Hui: Writing – review & editing. Li Qiang: Writing – review & editing. Du Jinglei: Conceptualization. Yu shiping: Writing – original draft. Zhou Shizhao: Writing – original draft. Wei Yingying: Writing – original draft. Ma Yihua: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
